features.name: Delete the gap between dev and prod
features.desc: The thing that actually burns people isn't a missing feature — it's that next dev and next start behave like two different machines. Caching off locally and on in prod. RSC that hydrates one way on your laptop and another after deploy. An edge runtime that only bites once it's live. Make local behave exactly like production by default. The bug you can reproduce on your own machine is the bug you fix in an hour instead of losing a weekend to.

features.name: One router, and the nerve to say so
features.desc: You ship the App Router and the Pages Router in the same framework and call it choice. It isn't choice — it's a fork in every tutorial, every Stack Overflow answer, and every new hire's first week. Pick App, write the codemod, set a deprecation date, and mean it. Two ways to do the one thing is the single most expensive feature you own.

features.name: Draw the server/client line on screen
features.desc: Right now I find out a component is client-only when it throws, and I fix it by pasting 'use client' until the red goes away. Show me the boundary instead — what runs where, what crosses it, what that costs in bytes. A line I can see is a line I stop tripping over.

more.name: Caching you opt into, not out of
more.desc: Three caches on by default, all invisible, is how a 'nothing changed' deploy serves last week's data to a paying customer. Flip the default: nothing is cached until I ask, and when I ask, I can see exactly what and why. Surprise is the opposite of trust, and trust is the whole job of a framework.

more.name: A first build that explains itself
more.desc: The first next build is a wall of green text and a quiet prayer. Give me one screen instead: every route, its size, static or dynamic, and what will cache. Make the cost of each page impossible to miss and people fix the fat one before it ships.

more.name: Errors that point at the line, not the layer
more.desc: Hydration mismatch, a stack trace, good luck. The framework already knows which component diverged and which prop changed — so say it, and name the one line to fix. The error you explain in plain words is the all-nighter nobody pulls.

more.name: Bless one way to fetch data
more.desc: Server-component fetch, server action, route handler, client library — four answers to one question, and a coin flip on every screen. Pick the one you'd teach a junior on day one, lead every doc with it, and let the others be advanced moves you grow into.

more.name: next.config that fits on one screen
more.desc: It's become a junk drawer of experimental flags and plugin wrappers nobody remembers enabling. Collapse it to one file, every option commented, defaults set to what you'd actually recommend. Config you can read in one sitting is config you can trust.

more.name: An honest door out
more.desc: When the magic fights a senior — and eventually it fights everyone — give them a clean way down a level that isn't 'eject and lose the framework forever.' People who feel trapped leave. People who can see an exit stay and keep building.

more.name: Run anywhere, not just one cloud
more.desc: The happy path quietly assumes one host, and 'deploy to my own box' becomes a community workaround instead of a first-class, documented road. A framework that only really sings on one platform isn't a framework — it's a sales funnel wearing one.

killed: a fourth way to fetch data
telemetry you have to opt out of
config for things that deserve one right default
magic that hides the call it's making
vendor edge assumptions baked into core

verdict.quote: I'll say the part everyone tiptoes around: you are the best way to ship React, and you are drowning the thing that made you great under everything you've bolted on since. Two routers. Caches I can't see. A server-client line I only learn by getting burned. When I cut Apple's products from three hundred and fifty down to ten, people called it reckless — it was the opposite. It's the only way the few things left get to be great. You have the talent. What you're missing is the no. Make dev match prod. Pick one router. Show me the boundary. Then delete the rest and stop calling the pile 'flexibility.' Do that and you're not just the React framework — you're the one nobody can argue with.
